title: Tunneling from an oscillating initial state in quantum mechanics
authors: Oliver Janssen, Matthew Kleban, Cameron Norton
abstract: We study the decay of general initial states out of a metastable potential well in quantum mechanics. We provide a closed-form expression for the probability current that tunnels through the barrier in terms of the resonant states into which the initial state can be decomposed. All ingredients in the equation are computed analytically to first subleading order in the semiclassical limit. Specializing to a coherently-oscillating initial state, we derive an approximation to the time-dependent decay rate and demonstrate its accuracy by comparing it to a numerical solution of the Schrödinger equation.
summary: This paper provides an analytical framework to calculate how oscillating quantum states tunnel through a barrier. It reveals that instead of a smooth decay, coherent states undergo tunneling in periodic, intense bursts of probability current. This work bridges the gap between stationary decay models and the dynamic tunneling seen in systems like Josephson junctions or cosmological bubble nucleation.
main_problem: Determining the time-dependent decay rate and tunneling probability of general, non-stationary initial states (specifically coherent/oscillating states) out of a metastable potential well.
main_result: The authors derive a closed-form expression for the probability current that captures interference effects and show that tunneling for coherent states occurs in narrow, periodic spikes when the wave packet reaches the classical turning point.
method: The study uses a resonant state expansion (Gamow-Siegert states) combined with semiclassical WKB approximations and the saddle-point method, validated against numerical solutions of the Schrödinger equation using a complex absorbing potential.
model_or_system: A quantum mechanical particle in a metastable potential well (such as a superconducting Josephson junction or a cosmological scalar field) separated from a free region by a potential barrier.
key_observables: Probability current (tunneling rate), time-dependent decay rate, and total leaked probability.
important_parameters: Semiclassical limit (large action), narrow-resonance regime, oscillation period, and the amplitude of the coherent state.
main_assumptions: The initial state is localized in the well; the expansion neglects the non-resonant continuum; the potential is approximated quadratically near the well bottom; and the results are valid for energies well below the barrier top.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the potential geometry and turning points; Figure 2 displays resonant states; Figure 3 compares different boundary conditions; Figure 4 and 5 demonstrate the accuracy of the proposed formula and the Gaussian shape of tunneling spikes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of non-vacuum decay, introduces the resonant state expansion and WKB framework, applies this to coherent states to derive periodic tunneling spikes, validates the analytical results with numerical simulations, and discusses the limits of the approximation.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics, as it provides an analytical tool to understand how phase coherence and initial state dynamics drive non-exponential, time-dependent decay in metastable systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A generic potential V assumed in our analysis. A well (left) is shown separated from an asymptotically free region (right) by a potential barrier. The dashed line shows a representative energy E. The classical turning points for this energy are c and a on the left and right in the well, and b on the right of the barrier. The start of the true vacuum, or free region, is denoted by xT.
Figure 2: The first 12 resonant states as found from the CAP (Complex Absorbing Potential) Hamiltonian.

Figure 3: The tunneling probability, 1 −P(t) (left) and probability current j(t) (right) comparing the time evolution of a coherent harmonic oscillator initial state with |α| = 1.1 via either CAP (Complex Absorbing Potential) evolution (black) or evolution with hard-wall boundary conditions (blue). For early-enough times, before the outgoing wave reaches the boundary, the plots are nearly indistinguishable.

Figure 4: The tunneling probability 1 −P(t) (top left) and probability current j(t) (top right) as functions of time, for a coherent simple harmonic oscillator initial state in the potential well with |α| = 1.1. The black solid lines show the direct numerical result from Crank-Nicolson time evolution with the CAP (Complex Absorbing Potential) Hamiltonian, the gray dashed lines show the formula of Eq. (18) evaluated numerically, and the blue dashed lines show the fully analytic WKB prediction using only the potential parameters. The residual panels (bottom) show the deviation of each approximation from the numerical result. The formula residual lies at the level of numerical precision,...

Figure 5: Numerical verification of Eqns. (33) and (42) for a coherent initial state with |α| = 1.1. Left: cumulative tunneling probability 1−P(t) for the first few oscillations obtained by integrating the numerical current (blue) and the saddle-point approximation to the current from Eq. (33) (dashed black). The horizontal red lines mark the total ∆P per oscillation cycle predicted by Eq. (42). Right: the probability current j(t) over one oscillation period centered at the tunneling peak φ = ωt −θα = 0. The saddle-point approximation from Eq. (33) (dashed black) matches the numerical current; the peak and Gaussian width agree to within 4%.
